request.question: Who created an American police procedural television series that starred the American actor Archie Kao as a regular?

sentences: Law & Order: Criminal Intent is an American police procedural television drama series set in New York City, where it was also primarily produced.
Created and produced by Dick Wolf and René Balcer, the series premiered on September 30, 2001, as the third series in Wolf's successful "Law & Order" franchise.
"Criminal Intent" focuses on the investigations of the Major Case Squad in a fictionalized version of the New York City Police Department set in New York City's One Police Plaza.
In the style of the original "Law & Order", episodes are often "ripped from the headlines" or loosely based on a real crime that received media attention.
Chicago P.D. is an American police procedural drama television series created by Dick Wolf and Matt Olmstead as the second installment of Dick Wolf's "Chicago" franchise.
The series premiered on NBC as a mid-season replacement on January 8, 2014.
The show follows the uniformed patrol officers and the Intelligence Unit of the 21st District of the Chicago Police Department as they pursue the perpetrators of the city's major street offences.
The eighth season of "" premiered on the USA Network in the United States on April 19, 2009.
It consisted of sixteen episodes, and concluded on August 9, 2009.
The day following each episode's broadcast on television, they are made available to purchase and download from the iTunes Store. ""
is an American police procedural television series set and filmed in New York City.
It is the second spin-off of the long-running crime drama "Law & Order", and was created by Dick Wolf and René Balcer.
"Law & Order: Criminal Intent" follows the New York City Police Department's Major Case Squad, which investigates high-profile murder cases.
Homicide: Life on the Street is an American police procedural television series chronicling the work of a fictional version of the Baltimore Police Department's Homicide Unit.
It ran for seven seasons (122 episodes) on NBC from January 31, 1993 to May 21, 1999, and was succeeded by "", which also acted as the "de facto" series finale.
The series was originally based on David Simon's book "".
Many of the characters and stories used throughout the show were based on events depicted in the book.
"Blue Bloods" is an American police procedural television series created by Robin Green and Mitchell Burgess.
It premiered on September 24, 2010 on CBS.
The series centers on the Reagans, a family of police officers in New York City.

Frank Reagan (Tom Selleck) is the police commissioner with three grown children working in law enforcement: the oldest, Danny (Donnie Wahlberg), a pragmatic detective; Erin (Bridget Moynahan), an assistant district attorney who prosecutes many of the offenders Danny apprehends; and Jamie (Will Estes), a Harvard Law School graduate and a patrol cop in the NYPD.
Frank's second son, Joseph, an NYPD detective, was killed in the line of duty while investigating a group of dirty cops nicknamed "The Blue Templar".
On March 25, 2016, the series was renewed for a seventh season, premiered on September 23, 2016.

CSI: NY (Crime Scene Investigation: New York, stylised as CSI: NY/) is an American police procedural television series that ran on CBS from September 22, 2004, to February 22, 2013, for a total of nine seasons and 197 original episodes.

The show follows the investigations of a team of NYPD forensic scientists and police officers identified as "Crime Scene Investigators" (instead of the actual title of "Crime Scene Unit Forensic Technicians" (CSU)) as they unveil the circumstances behind mysterious and unusual deaths, as well as other crimes.
The series is an indirect spin-off from the veteran series "" and a direct spin-off from "", during an episode in which several of the "CSI: NY" characters made their first appearances.
It is the third series in the "CSI" franchise.
, an American police procedural television series, premiered on September 20, 1999 on NBC.

Created by Dick Wolf, it is the first spin-off of "Law & Order" and follows the detectives of a fictionalized version of the New York City Police Department's Special Victims Unit, which investigates sexually based offenses.
"SVU" originally aired on Monday nights at 9pm/8c EST, but it was moved to Friday nights at 10pm/9c after the ninth episode.
Showrunner Robert Palm felt too disturbed by the subject matter and left after the season's conclusion.
"" is an American police procedural television series set and filmed in New York City.
It is the second spin-off of the long-running crime drama "Law & Order".

"Law & Order: Criminal Intent" follows a distinct division of the New York City Police Department: the Major Case Squad, which investigates high-profile murder cases, such as those involving VIPs, local government officials and employees, the financial industry, and the art world.

Unlike the other series in the "Law & Order" franchise "Law & Order: Criminal Intent" gives significant attention to the actions and motives of the criminals, rather than primarily focusing on the police investigation and trial prosecution.
Episodes do not usually contain trials, and often end in confessions rather than plea bargains or verdicts.
"Miami Vice" is an American police procedural television series which was broadcast for five seasons on the National Broadcasting Company (NBC) between 1984 and 1990.

Starring Philip Michael Thomas, Don Johnson, Edward James Olmos, Olivia Brown, Saundra Santiago, John Diehl and Michael Talbott, it focuses on the lives of two undercover Metro-Dade police officers, Ricardo Tubbs (Thomas) and James "Sonny" Crockett (Johnson).
The series was created by Anthony Yerkovich, with Michael Mann and Dick Wolf serving as executive producers.
Archie David Kao (born December 14, 1969) is an American actor.
He is best known to American audiences for series regulars roles on "Chicago P.D.", "Power Rangers Lost Galaxy" as well as long-running hit "".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who created an American police procedural television series that starred the American actor Archie Kao as a regular?
hop1_docs: Archie Kao | Archie David Kao (born December 14, 1969) is an American actor.  He is best known to American audiences for series regulars roles on "Chicago P.D.", "Power Rangers Lost Galaxy" as well as long-running hit "".

The Chinese Detective | The Chinese Detective is a British television police procedural drama series, first transmitted by the BBC between 1981 and 1982.  The series was created by Ian Kennedy Martin, who had previously devised "The Sweeney" and "Juliet Bravo".  The series starred British Chinese actor David Yip as Detective Sergeant John Ho, the first Chinese lead actor in a British television drama series.  The series offered traditional police procedural storylines in a setting of occasional prejudice and distrust within the police force, and the prejudice displayed by those Ho encounters whilst doing his job.  Like many other television detectives of the time, Ho was something of a maverick, often using unorthodox methods to solve crimes.  The series was set in and around London's docklands, before redevelopment began in the 1980s.

Angie Tribeca | Angie Tribeca is an American comedy television series created by Steve and Nancy Carell.  The series, a satire of police procedural shows, stars Rashida Jones as the titular police detective Angie Tribeca.  It also stars Hayes MacArthur, Jere Burns, Deon Cole, and Andree Vermeulen in supporting roles.

Jay Seals | Jay Seals (born March 23, 1976) is an American actor, known for his recurring role in the television drama "Mad Men" on AMC from 2010 to 2015.  Some of his other credits include the 2012 American television police procedural fantasy drama "Awake", as a police tech, Nat, who is considered to be a "tech-savvy cop".  He also appeared in numerous other television series and films.

Monk (TV series) | Monk is an American comedy-drama detective mystery television series created by Andy Breckman and starring Tony Shalhoub as the eponymous character, Adrian Monk.  It originally ran from 2002 to 2009 and is primarily a police procedural series, and also exhibits comic and dramatic tones in its exploration of the main characters' personal lives.  The series was produced by Mandeville Films and Touchstone Television in association with Universal Television.

Hannah Britten | Hannah Britten is a fictional protagonist in the American police procedural drama television series "Awake".  She is portrayed by Laura Allen.  The character first appeared in "Pilot" and last appeared in the series finale "Turtles All the Way Down".  Hannah was created and designed by series creator and executive producer Kyle Killen after he created and developed the series "Lone Star" for the Fox network.

Erik Estrada | Henry Enrique "Erik" Estrada (born March 16, 1949) is an American actor, voice actor, and police officer known for his co-starring lead role in the police drama television series "CHiPs", which ran from 1977 to 1983.  He later became known for his work in Spanish-language "telenovelas", his appearances in reality television shows and infomercials and as a regular voice on the Adult Swim series "Sealab 2021" as well as the movie "Cool Cat Saves The Kids".
response.summary_1: Passage [1] establishes the bridge: Archie Kao "is best known to American audiences for series regulars roles on 'Chicago P.D.'." Missing fact: the creator(s) of the American police procedural series "Chicago P.D." Next hop / retrieval direction: fetch the "Chicago P.D." article or authoritative credits and look for the "created by" / creator credit (search terms: "Chicago P.D. created by" or "Chicago P.D. creator").
hop2_docs: Chicago Woman's Club | The Chicago Woman's Club was formed in 1876 by women in Chicago who were interested in "self and social improvement."  The club was notable for creating educational opportunities in the Chicago region and helped create the first juvenile court in the United States.  The group was primarily made up of wealthy and middle-class white women, with physicians, lawyers and university professors playing "prominent roles."  The club often worked towards social and educational reform in Chicago.  It also hosted talks by prominent women, including artists and suffragists.

Chicago Innovation Awards | The Chicago Innovation Awards was created by the Chicago Sun-Times and Kuczmarski & Associates in 2002.  Each year the Awards recognize 10 Chicago area businesses, nonprofits, and government organizations that develop the year’s most innovative new products and services.

Antonio Dawson | Antonio Dawson, portrayed by Jon Seda, is a fictional character in the "Chicago" franchise and is a main character in "Chicago P.D." and "Chicago Justice".  He was introduced as a recurring character on "Chicago Fire" before the spin-off "Chicago P.D." was created.  He was previously the second in command of the Intelligence Unit of the Chicago Police Department (CPD) and regularly appears on "Chicago Fire" as he is the older brother of Gabriela "Gabby" Dawson, a paramedic-turned-firefighter at Firehouse 51.

Chicago News Cooperative | The Chicago News Cooperative was a not-for-profit, Chicago-based cooperative that was created to produce news stories about Chicago for various media organizations.  It was formed in November 2009, distributed its content to "The New York Times" and shut down in February 2012.

Amasa Wright | Amasa Wright (1787–1861) originally owned the land under the Chicago loop.  He subdivided the land to create the city of Chicago.  There is a picture of him in the museum at the top of the Willis Tower in Chicago.

Chicago Evening Post | The Chicago Evening Post was a daily newspaper published in Chicago, Illinois, from March 1, 1886, until October 29, 1932, when it was absorbed by the "Chicago Daily News".  The newspaper was founded as a penny paper during the technological paradigm shift created by linotype; it failed when the Great Depression struck.

Chicago Hope | Chicago Hope is an American medical drama television series, created by David E. Kelley.  It ran on CBS from September 18, 1994, to May 4, 2000.  The series is set in a fictional private charity hospital in Chicago, Illinois.
response.summary_2: Archie Kao is best known to American audiences for a series-regular role on Chicago P.D.; the provided passages do not state who "created" Chicago P.D., so the series' creator(s) are not confirmed here.
